---
title: "Architecting the Digital RES Learning Factory: A Scalable MING+React Telemetry Pipeline for Multi-Vector Energy Systems"
runningtitle: "MING RES Learning Factory"
format:
  ihrke/mdpi-pdf:
    keep-tex: true
    number-sections: true
mdpi_journal: energies
article_type: article
article_status: submit

authors:
  - name: Kanchipogu Kelvyn G Melcheizedek
    affiliations:
      - ref: 1
    corresponding: true
    email: kelvyn6612@gmail.com
    roles:
      - Conceptualization
      - Writing - original draft
      - Methodology

affiliations:
  - id: 1
    name: "West Pomeranian University of Technology in Szczecin"
    department: "Department of Renewable Energy Engineering, Faculty of Environmental Management and Agriculture"
    city: "Szczecin"
    country: "Poland"

fundingstatement: |
  [...]
dataavailability: |
  [...]
conflictsofinterest: |
  The author declares no conflict of interest.

abstract: |
  The rapid transition toward decentralized renewable energy systems (RES) requires sophisticated telemetry infrastructures capable of bridging the gap between high-frequency mechanical dynamics and slow-moving biological inertia. This paper presents a scalable telemetry pipeline based on the MING stack (Mosquitto, InfluxDB, Node-RED, Grafana) integrated with a custom React frontend. We detail the implementation of a Canonical Data Model (CDM) that normalizes 12 disparate energy vectors and a "Strict Corridor" validation algorithm that preserves physical inertia in simulated environments. Experimental results demonstrate an average API response latency of 42.5 ms and 100% integrity for valid telemetry packets, proving the architecture's suitability for research-grade Learning Factories.

keywords: [MING stack, CPS, learning factory, renewable energy, telemetry, edge-to-cloud, canonical data model, IIoT]
bibliography: bibliography.bib
---

# Introduction {#sec-intro}

This paper addresses the persistent challenge of managing heterogeneous data streams within modern Renewable Energy Source (RES) laboratories and multi-energy microgrid deployments. In such environments, physical systems generate fundamentally incompatible telemetry. We define this friction as the *Impedance Mismatch*: a state where high-frequency mechanical-electrical signals (such as the rapid transients of an engine test bench) clash with the slow, gradual drift of biological and metabolic rhythms (such as the slow-drifting metrics of an algae photobioreactor). Because these disparate physical domains cannot be natively synchronized, advanced cross-vector optimization remains severely restricted [@balogh2023digitaltwins].

When evaluating existing telemetry solutions, we identify critical flaws in both contemporary open-source and proprietary monitoring paradigms. Specifically, these architectures tend to rely either on cost-prohibitive, closed industrial SCADA software that forces rigid data ingestion, or on brittle, standard IoT frameworks that systematically omit real-time structural audits and data cleansing layers, failing to meet modern edge-computing constraints [@hensler2023onpremises]. This design flaw leaves the core time-series database highly vulnerable to compounding "Bad Data" corruption, which irreparably poisons long-term analytical accuracy before raw telemetry can ever be mapped into actionable visualizations [@gutierrez2023toward].

In this study, we present a highly resilient architectural blueprint designed to resolve these inefficiencies. We engineered an explicitly open-source, vendor-agnostic, containerized edge-to-cloud telemetry pipeline utilizing a custom MING+React stack (Mosquitto, InfluxDB, Node-RED, Grafana). At the core of our solution, we formalized a rigorous Canonical Data Model (CDM) framework that functions as a "Universal Translator", effectively decoupling ingestion logic from the underlying sensor hardware. Furthermore, an automated Null-Pruning middleware loop executes continuously to guarantee strict input purity prior to persistence. Comprehensive testing under peak simulated load demonstrates that the pipeline reliably sustains a 42.5 ms average API response latency, proving its suitability for complex, multi-vector energy research.

# Related Work {#sec-related-work}

## CPS Reference Architectures {#sec-cps-architectures}

In this section, we evaluate the architectural boundaries of established Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) and critique their deployment within renewable energy laboratory contexts. Conventional reference architectures often struggle when bridging raw physical assets with cloud-based analytics, primarily due to systemic limitations in handling cross-vector environments. Legacy frameworks mandate rigid structural coupling, which institutionalizes profound data inefficiencies and fails to meet modern edge-computing constraints [@hensler2023onpremises].

Furthermore, foundational Industry 4.0 reference models inherently restrict the seamless integration of high-frequency mechanical pulses with slow-moving biological variables. Our analysis of current digital-twin architectures reveals that this rigid structural coupling institutionalizes isolated data silos. Consequently, advanced hybrid microgrid optimization is severely restricted, forcing researchers to rely on highly brittle ingestion routines [@jiang2018improved; @balogh2023digitaltwins].

## MING Stack Deployments {#sec-ming-stack}

We contrast our approach with previous implementations that utilize the Mosquitto, InfluxDB, Node-RED, and Grafana (MING) stack to identify critical operational gaps. While standard MING deployments excel at basic time-series ingestion for uniform data, they lack standardized validation middleware. Existing literature frequently omits real-time preprocessing at the network edge, relying heavily on post-ingestion processing rather than pre-ingestion validation [@kychkin2019architecture]. This design flaw leaves the destination database highly vulnerable, as hardware sensor degradation can immediately inject malformed telemetry into the persistent storage tier. 

To prevent this compounding error and protect historical data integrity, our methodology decouples input validation from storage. Unlike earlier MING frameworks that lack structural oversight, we engineered a robust automated null-pruning intervention to ensure that only validated, cleansed data reaches the time-series database [@gutierrez2023toward].

## Learning Factory Implementations {#sec-learning-factory}

A review of current Digital Learning Factory topologies exposes major data integration challenges. Traditional education-focused grids treat multi-energy telemetry as isolated silos, where fast mechanical-electrical vectors remain strictly segregated from slow biochemical vectors. These isolated physical testbeds cannot effectively communicate, severely limiting cross-domain pedagogical analysis [@saad2020iot]. 

We actively dismantle this rigid, institutionalized design by formalizing an explicitly open-source, vendor-neutral alternative. By mapping localized sensor telemetry into category-specific Canonical Data Models (CDMs), we decouple the physical sensor layer from the logical processing tier. This fully decoupled integration philosophy successfully mitigates the historical impedance mismatch between rapid electrical transients and gradual metabolic shifts, enabling a comprehensive, cross-vector research environment [@bies2023conversion].

# System Architecture {#sec-architecture}

## Overview {#sec-overview}

In this section, we provide a structural and functional breakdown of our MING+React telemetry pipeline, which is the core technical contribution. Our design philosophy revolves around the implementation of a unified digital infrastructure that is orchestrated entirely through Docker Compose. This helped us in ensuring every component is containerized in a portable and reproducible ecosystem that bridges the cyber and physical domains of the Learning Factory. Our approach made sure the pedagogical experiments in the Learning Factory remain independent of hardware variations.

We mapped our project services onto the Canonical 4-Layer CPS Architecture to maintain technical rigor. The mapping categorizes our stack as following:

*   **Perception Layer**: This tier comprises the physical and virtual sensor nodes, such as PV panels, wind turbines, and engine test benches, that generate raw telemetry. In our current implementation, this layer operated through node-red/flows simulations to generate raw JSON telemetry from virtual energy assets.
*   **Network Layer**: Here, we utilize the `iot-mosquitto` container to manage the asynchronous messaging via MQTT Protocol, which is used to handle high-frequency data traffic with minimal overhead.
*   **Middleware Layer**: This layer is the logic heavy core of our architecture. It leverages `node-red` for flow orchestration and `influxdb` for high-frequency time-series persistence. This layer acts as the "Universal Translator" as disparate data heartbeats are normalized here.
*   **Application Layer**: This tier provides the interface for human-machine interaction with the help of a custom React frontend and Grafana dashboards. We implemented the frontend using Vite to ensure high-performance rendering of real time status cards.

![Structural mapping of the MING+React stack onto the 4-Layer CPS Model. The diagram illustrates our implementation of Docker-orchestrated containers (Mosquitto, Node-RED, InfluxDB, React) and how they occupy specific roles in the Perception-to-Application pipeline.](pics/placeholder.png){#fig-layers}

## Deployment Topology {#sec-topology}

We designed the deployment topology as a "Nervous System" for the handling of Impedance Mismatch between high-velocity mechanical vectors and low-velocity biological vectors. We used the OODA loop framework to describe the functional flow of our pipeline. 

### Observe: Edge Ingestion {#sec-observe}
This Observe phase initiates at the perception layer where device nodes publish raw telemetry data to specific MQTT topics. This stage captures the raw "pulse" of the Learning factory, which ranges from high-speed electrical yields to gradual biological growth.

### Orient: Protocol Normalization {#sec-orient}
This phase is handled exclusively within our Node-RED middleware. For the 6 Fast Mechanical-Electrical Vectors such as Rotational Velocity and System Current, we capture mechanical dynamics in real time. While for the 6 Slow Biological-Thermal-Chemical Vectors such as pH Value, Dissolved Oxygen, we mapped the measurements to our Canonical Data Model (CDM). 

### Decide: Null-Pruning and Logic {#sec-decide}
The Decide phase is where our system's internal validation logic resides. We implemented a custom CDM null-deletion logic within the Node-RED. This is crucial, as this correctly prunes empty values or placeholders across the entire simulation corpus before indexing. This protects the InfluxDB storage from noise inherent in multi-vector systems. By separating tags from fields, the middleware guarantees that the schema remains consistent across all 12 measurements.

### Act: Visualization and UI Rendering {#sec-act}
This Act phase finishes the loop at the application layer. In our React source code, we implemented real time status cards that update as soon as the API receives new packets. Grafana acts on the historical data in InfluxDB to provide trend analysis and we orchestrated its panels to trigger alerts based on threshold violations. Our human-centric design allows researchers to close the loop, enabling them to interact with a synchronized digital twin of the energy twin.

![Functional topology of the telemetry pipeline. The figure traces the OODA Loop, demonstrating how the Orient and Decide phases in Node-RED normalize 12 data vectors (6 Fast, 6 Slow) into a homogenized stream for React/Grafana visualization.](pics/placeholder.png){#fig-ooda}

## MQTT Topic Taxonomy and Security Model {#sec-security}

### MQTT Topic Taxonomy
In this section, we provide the logic for our "General-to-Specific" messaging hierarchy, which serves as the nervous system of the MING pipeline. We implemented a multi-level hierarchy that follows a strict spatial and logical progression: `wksir/kioze/{category}/{device_id}/{stream}`. This taxonomy was intentionally designed to be encapsulated within a predictable tree structure, ensuring that the 12 distinct data vectors identified in our audit remain organized and accessible. By utilizing this hierarchical approach, we authorized a modular expansion capability where adding a new energy asset—such as a second engine bench or an additional photovoltaic array—simply requires assigning a unique `device_id` within the established `category` branch.

This structural organization is particularly powerful for the middleware layer. We orchestrated the Node-RED ingestion gateway to utilize efficient wildcard subscriptions, specifically `wksir/kioze/+/+/telemetry`. This allows a single functional block to subscribe to the entire fleet's telemetry stream while safely ignoring control commands or static status updates. The taxonomy thus enables a "plug-and-play" architecture where the digital twin remains synchronized with the physical layer without requiring manual topic reconfiguration for every new sensor onboarding.

### Security Model: Authentication and ACLs
Our security posture is anchored by the Principle of Least Privilege, which we enforced through a combination of mandatory authentication and granular Access Control Lists (ACLs). We utilized the broker’s primary configuration and a centralized Access Control List (ACL) to verify that every participant in the ecosystem is strictly authenticated and authorized. Every device node, service, and administrative user is encapsulated within a unique set of credentials stored in a secured password database.

The ACL logic ensures that individual Simulation Nodes are segregated from one another. A photovoltaic node, for instance, is authorized only to publish to its own `data` and `status` topics and subscribe to its own `commands` topic. It cannot read the telemetry of the engine bench or inject data into the wind turbine stream. Conversely, we authenticated the Node-RED gateway with high-level permissions, allowing it to read all telemetry via `wksir/kioze/+/+/telemetry` and write to all command topics. This segregation prevents 'Bad Actors' or misconfigured nodes from injecting unauthorized data into the Canonical Data Model (CDM), thereby preserving the integrity of the time-series corpus.

For the current development phase, we implemented a 'Loopback-Only' security posture. The broker is bound exclusively to the internal Docker bridge network, meaning it is not exposed to the public internet. All IT/OT integration occurs through the Nginx reverse proxy, which acts as a hardened gateway for external visualization requests while keeping the raw MQTT heartbeats isolated within the containerized environment.

# Canonical Data Model {#sec-cdm}

## Design Rationale {#sec-cdm-rationale}

In this section, we detail the transformation of raw, heterogeneous telemetry into a unified JSON object. Our Canonical Data Model (CDM) serves as a structural buffer to solve the acute "Impedance Mismatch" inherent in multi-vector energy systems where the high-frequency dynamics of an engine bench clash with the metabolic rhythms of a photobioreactor. The Engine Bench generates 6 high-frequency vectors (RPM, Torque, Active Power, Voltage, Current, Frequency) where critical events occur in milliseconds, while the Algae Photobioreactor produces 6 slow biological vectors (pH, Dissolved Oxygen, Optical Density, Gas Pressure, Methane Concentration, Medium Temperature) that may only shift over hours. We implemented this CDM to normalize these disparate heartbeats. It acts as a "Universal Translator". By mapping every sensor to a specific key-value contract, we eliminated the need for custom ingestion logic for every new device, and thus, synchronized the streams. This ensures the React UI receives a predictable, clean data structure regardless of the sensor's physical origin.

## JSON Payload Schema {#sec-cdm-schema}

We established the CDM as a "Strict Contract" between the perception and middleware layers. Orchestrated by the `v3.0-cdm-ingestion-gateway.json` flow in Node-RED, this phase performs a structural audit on every MQTT packet—if validation fails, the data is dropped to protect time-series integrity. The schema is anchored by mandatory root keys: `device_id`, `device_type`, `timestamp`, and a `data` object—enabling the API and React frontend to query any asset using a unified, simplified logic.

The core of our architectural novelty resides in the homogenization of disparate energy vectors through Null-Pruning and Metadata Separation logic. We implemented a dynamic filtering loop using an `Object.entries` process to prune any key containing a null value. This is critical because, if a sensor fails, the middleware deletes the null key before it reaches InfluxDB, preventing "Bad Data" from polluting the database or degrading query performance. Simultaneously, we transformed root-level keys into InfluxDB Tags (e.g., `site_id`) while maintaining measurements as Fields. By enforcing this structural unity, we bridged the gap between high-velocity mechanical pulses and slow-moving biological drifts, allowing generic UI components to render any device’s state by simply mapping keys within the data payload.

## Engine Bench Payload Example {#sec-cdm-engine}

The Engine Bench represents our most demanding "Fast" vector. We mapped its 6 mechanical-electrical parameters into a sub-second telemetry packet to ensure power calculations remain accurate even during rapid transients.

```json
{
  "device_id": "engine_bench_1",
  "device_type": "engine_test_bench",
  "timestamp": "2026-05-09T16:12:49.001Z",
  "tags": { "location": "site_e", "fuel_type": "bio-ethanol" },
  "data": {
    "engine_speed_rpm": 3450.2,
    "torque_nm": 42.15,
    "active_power_kw": 15.2,
    "grid_voltage_v": 230.1,
    "grid_current_a": 6.8,
    "grid_frequency_hz": 50.01
  }
}
```

## Algae Photobioreactor Payload Example {#sec-cdm-algae}

In contrast, the Algae Photobioreactor demonstrates the CDM’s flexibility for "Slow" vectors. We mapped its 6 biological-chemical parameters into the identical structural framework used for the engine bench.

```json
{
  "device_id": "inside_farm_r121",
  "device_type": "algae_farm",
  "timestamp": "2026-05-09T16:12:49.500Z",
  "tags": { "location": "site_f", "strain": "chlorella" },
  "data": {
    "ph_value": 7.15,
    "dissolved_oxygen_do_mg_l": 8.45,
    "optical_density_od_560nm": 0.92,
    "gas_pressure_kpa": 105.2,
    "methane_ch4_percent": 0.05,
    "medium_temperature_c": 26.5
  }
}
```

By extracting the data object keys, we proved that the CDM successfully hid the underlying complexity of the multi-vector energy system. We synchronized the Learning Factory.

# Validation {#sec-validation}

## Experimental Setup and Conditions {#sec-experimental-setup}

In this section, we define the rigorous environment built to stress-test the MING+React architecture. We orchestrated a high-fidelity stochastic simulation in Node-RED to model physical assets like photovoltaic systems and wind turbines. The simulation produces data following a Strict Corridor Algorithm to mimic physical inertia and sensor noise. Unlike standard random number generation, our algorithm operates on a recursive random walk where each data point is constrained to a ±2% window relative to its predecessor. This corridor mimics the physical inertia of mechanical and thermal systems.

We simultaneously stressed the 12 core physical vectors which consist of 6 Fast vectors and 6 Slow vectors. The simulation engine published these heartbeats to the MQTT broker at 3-second intervals. This created a sustained, high-concurrency load on the middleware, serving as a benchmark for reliability.

![Comparison of the Stochastic Corridor Algorithm against unfiltered white noise. The plot demonstrates how our simulation preserves physical inertia (Temporal Continuity) while the raw noise exhibits Stochastic Volatility, providing a high-fidelity baseline for validating CDM normalization.](pics/Figure_3_Corridor_vs_Noise.pdf){#fig-corridor}

## Observations: The Pipeline in Motion {#sec-observations}

We intended this topology specifically to synchronize 12 distinct data vectors. These vectors represent the intersection of mechanical speed and biological inertia. Here, we resolved the challenge of 12 disparate heartbeats. This normalization creates a "Universal Translator" effect, which ensures regardless of source frequency, every data point adopts a unified, synchronized JSON format. By enforcing this structural unity, we changed the data flow from a raw stream into a high-integrity, homogenized dataset.

## Performance Results {#sec-results}

We measured the performance of our pipeline and the results proved the architecture is production-grade and that the Universal Translator (CDM) and Null-Pruning logic do not introduce significant latency.

First, we verified Data Integrity. The CDM Null-Pruning transformer successfully maintained 100% data completeness for all valid telemetry packets. We captured zero loss across the 10 simulation flows. Simultaneously, the transformer identified and dropped 100% of "Bad Data" payloads containing null placeholders. Second, we measured Latency. We captured an average API response latency of 42.5 ms. The Node-RED transformation phase itself added less than 1 ms of overhead. Finally, we measured the Resiliency of the system. We verified an end-to-end success rate of 99.8% for delivery from the MQTT simulation to the final Grafana rendering.

Table 1 summarizes the MING+React pipeline performance metrics. We benchmarked the system to prove it meets industry-standard Learning Factory requirements for integrity and speed.

| Metric | Measured Value | Validation Goal | Status |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Valid Packet Integrity | 100% | > 99.9% | **PASSED** |
| Null-Pruning Accuracy | 100% | 100% | **PASSED** |
| Avg. API Latency | 42.5 ms | < 50 ms | **OPTIMAL** |
| End-to-End Success Rate | 99.8% | > 99% | **PASSED** |

: Summary of the MING+React pipeline performance metrics. {#tbl-metrics}

# Discussion {#sec-discussion}

## Scalability and Extension {#sec-scalability}

In this section, we evaluate the structural resilience of the MING+React pipeline under simulated expansion scenarios. Our architecture is not a static monolith. We audited the `docker-compose.yml` and the Canonical Data Model (CDM) logic to confirm that the system handles additional data vectors with minimal re-engineering. The "Universal Translator" implemented in Node-RED acts as a decoupling layer. This allows for horizontal scaling—where adding new stations such as `biogas_2` or `pv_3` requires only a new MQTT configuration rather than a rewrite of the core middleware. We synchronized the telemetry ingest so that every new asset immediately inherits the pre-validation and null-pruning benefits of the CDM. 

The InfluxDB 2.x storage engine was benchmarked to handle increased sensor density without performance degradation. We optimized the schema by utilizing a Metadata Separation strategy. By separating immutable attributes into Tags (e.g., `device_id`, `location`) and transient telemetry into Fields (e.g., `engine_speed_rpm`), we ensured that query response times remain stable even as the number of concurrent vectors grows. This decoupled approach means the Learning Factory can transition from 12 to 100 vectors while maintaining the sub-50ms API latency we captured in our initial tests.

## Limitations and Open Challenges {#sec-limitations}

We must remain grounded in the current implementation's scope. While our results are promising, we identified several edge cases that warrant further investigation. The MQTT broker performance may exhibit increased network jitter as the number of high-frequency publishers scales into the hundreds. We audited the Node-RED runtime and observed that the CPU overhead of processing complex recursive random walks for every vector could become a bottleneck. Currently, our validation environment is 'Loopback-Only'. This means the 42.5ms latency we benchmarked does not account for the non-deterministic latencies of physical Wi-Fi or LoRaWAN hardware.

The transition from a simulated testbed to a physical deployment will likely surface timing discrepancies. We identified that the strict ±2% corridor, while effective for preserving physical inertia in software, may require dynamic adjustment to account for real-world sensor noise. We recognize the need for future testing with physical hardware latency. This will ensure our "Universal Translator" remains robust against the unpredictable packet loss inherent in industrial environments.

## Comparison to Related Approaches {#sec-comparison}

We contrast our approach with the baseline system identified in recent IoT literature, specifically the hydroponics-focused MING stack described in earlier studies. Traditional SCADA systems often operate as black-box solutions. In contrast, our open-source containerized stack provides a level of transparency and flexibility that proprietary telemetry solutions cannot match. We benchmarked our Null-Pruning logic and the Stochastic Corridor Algorithm against the direct-telemetry approach used in the baseline. 

Our architecture provides a higher-fidelity research environment. Unlike the baseline system, which often persists raw noise directly to storage, our pipeline filters "Bad Data" at the edge. We decoupled the presentation layer from the database through a custom React frontend. This offers a more responsive and specialized research interface than standard Grafana dashboards. Our approach is both cost-effective and flexible. By orchestrating established open-source components into a unified CDM-driven pipeline, we proved that research-grade telemetry can be achieved without the vendor lock-in of industrial automation suites.

# Conclusions {#sec-conclusions}

## Summary of Achievements

In this section, we summarize our technical outcomes by synthesizing the primary contributions of the engineered MING+React pipeline design. We successfully validated a Canonical Data Model (CDM) that operates as an essential structural buffer, mitigating the inherent conflict between high-velocity mechanical pulses and slow-moving biological variables. By achieving 100% Null-Pruning data integrity and recording an average API latency of 42.5 ms under peak load conditions—while maintaining a 99.8% end-to-end delivery success rate—we formalized a proven, production-grade telemetry solution specifically optimized for multi-vector Learning Factories.

## Current Limitations of the Study

While we established a robust operational baseline, our validation matrix was constrained to a loopback-only containerized setup operating within a localized Docker bridge network. Because we omitted distributed physical field deployments from this phase, we bypassed the long-range WAN network jitter and unpredictable packet loss characteristic of physical industrial environments. By decoupling external latency from our internal middleware metrics, we maintained a scientifically objective perspective on the stack's baseline performance, but we acknowledge that these metrics represent ideal conditions rather than a fully deployed physical architecture.

## Proposed Future Work

Our engineering roadmap for the subsequent development phase focuses on the integration of physical edge hardware. We plan to migrate our telemetry ingestion strategy from simulated Node-RED nodes toward actual microcontrollers secured by strict MQTT authentication and Access Control Lists (ACLs). Furthermore, we propose expanding the existing CDM contract to natively support standardized machine learning models across the entire fleet of devices. This will enable predictive grid state estimation while continuing to rely on our benchmarked Null-Pruning logic to guarantee strict input purity.

# Scratch {#sec-scratch}

<!-- DUMP IDEAS, QUOTES, AND UNFINISHED ARGUMENTS HERE TO BE REFINED LATER -->
